title: Verify and Cite Practical Claims
summary: Evaluate practical claims by checking source quality, recency, evidence, and applicability before acting.
plain_language: Before you trust a claim, check who said it, when they said it, what evidence they show, and whether it fits your situation.

payload.performance_statement: Assess a practical claim, cite the supporting evidence, and explain whether the claim is usable in a real decision.
steps_summary: State the claim in one sentence.
Identify the original source, date, and evidence offered.
Compare at least two independent sources or one primary source and one reviewer.
Mark what is known, unknown, and locally variable.
Record a decision with a citation trail.
success_criteria: The worksheet names the claim, source, date, and evidence quality.
The learner can distinguish primary from secondary support.
The final note states whether to act, wait, test, or reject.
failure_modes: Treating popularity as proof.
Ignoring dates and using outdated guidance.
Failing to separate general advice from local conditions.
payload.safety_boundary: This skill does not authorize high-risk action; when consequences are significant, escalate to qualified local guidance and primary sources.

title: Measurement and Units for Practical Work
summary: Understand quantities, conversions, estimation, and tolerances so plans and calculations use comparable units.
plain_language: If you cannot measure it in the same units, you cannot compare it or size it correctly.

payload.definition: A shared language of quantity that allows materials, loads, water, time, and space to be compared without confusion.
key_questions: What unit is appropriate for this decision?
How do I convert between common household and technical units?
misconceptions: Precision and accuracy are the same thing.
Bigger numbers always mean more capacity regardless of unit.
Approximation is useless unless it is exact.
formulas_or_rules: Choose one working unit before calculating.
Write units next to every number.
Estimate first, then calculate, then sense-check.

title: Map Household and Community Systems
summary: Map inputs, outputs, dependencies, and failure points in a system so you can see what matters first.
plain_language: Draw the system before you try to fix the system.

payload.performance_statement: Create a clear map of a household or local system that shows how resources flow, where bottlenecks exist, and what fails first.
steps_summary: Choose a system boundary such as water, food, shelter, or power.
List inputs, outputs, storage points, dependencies, and single points of failure.
Mark critical nodes, backup paths, and failure consequences.
Rank which dependencies are easy, medium, or hard to replace.
Convert the map into a one-page operating view.
success_criteria: The map shows inputs, outputs, and at least one likely failure path.
Critical dependencies are visible and ranked.
Another person can read the map and understand the system.
failure_modes: Drawing a process list instead of a system map.
Ignoring hidden dependencies such as fuel, internet, or weather.
Making the diagram so detailed that it cannot guide action.
payload.safety_boundary: This activity is observational and planning-oriented; do not test failure scenarios on live systems unless it is safe and reversible.

title: Use AI for Grounded Research and Synthesis
summary: Use AI to summarize, compare, localize, and draft while keeping claims tied to checkable sources and local constraints.
plain_language: Use AI like a fast research assistant and translator, not like a prophet.

payload.performance_statement: Use AI to speed up explanation and comparison while preserving a verifiable chain back to sources, dates, and local constraints.
inputs: local context such as budget or climate
steps_summary: Frame the task in plain language and define the decision to be made.
Ask AI to summarize and compare sources with explicit citations or references.
Cross-check the high-risk or time-sensitive claims against primary or recent sources.
Ask AI to localize the plan by budget, region, housing type, or available tools.
Save the final decision note with sources and caveats.
success_criteria: The output names which claims were source-checked.
The learner can explain where AI helped and where human verification took over.
The final note is adapted to a real local scenario.
failure_modes: Accepting confident wording as evidence.
Prompting for conclusions without defining constraints.
Forgetting to verify recency, law, code, or safety boundaries.
payload.safety_boundary: AI outputs are draft material; do not use them as sole authority for code, medicine, legal matters, or high-risk technical action.

title: Build a Household Resilience Inventory
summary: Create a baseline inventory of needs, dependencies, tools, contacts, and vulnerabilities for a household.
plain_language: Write down what the household depends on before an outage or disruption writes it down for you.

payload.goal: Produce a single reference packet that shows what the household needs to stay functional for 72 hours and two weeks.
acceptance_criteria: The inventory covers water, food, shelter, sanitation, communications, medicines, transport, and power.
Every item has a quantity, location, and owner or steward where relevant.
The packet highlights the top five vulnerabilities and the easiest next improvements.

title: Store Potable Water Safely
summary: Store potable water in usable quantities, containers, and intervals so it stays available and identifiable.
plain_language: Clean water only helps if you can store enough of it, find it fast, and trust the container.

payload.performance_statement: Calculate water quantities for a time window, choose appropriate containers, and label and rotate stored water correctly.
steps_summary: Estimate minimum and preferred daily water quantities per person and use case.
Match the total volume to actual containers and storage locations.
Label date, source, intended use, and rotation interval.
Separate drinking water from utility water if using both.
Record the plan in a storage table.
success_criteria: The plan covers at least one minimum-duration target.
Containers are identified, dated, and assigned to uses.
Storage locations are realistic for the dwelling.
failure_modes: Using unknown or contaminated containers.
Storing too little water for heat, pets, or sanitation needs.
Failing to label and rotate containers.
payload.safety_boundary: Use only appropriate containers and local health guidance; storage does not replace proper treatment when source quality is uncertain.

title: Select an Appropriate Water Treatment Method
summary: Understand treatment methods and match them to the likely contaminants, water source, and available equipment.
plain_language: Different water problems need different fixes; no single treatment method solves everything.

payload.definition: A decision framework for matching likely water hazards to suitable treatment methods and limits.
key_questions: What problem am I treating: particles, microbes, chemicals, or salinity?
What does this treatment remove well, poorly, or not at all?
What are the capacity, maintenance, and failure signs of the method?
misconceptions: Clear water is necessarily safe water.
formulas_or_rules: Multiple barriers are often stronger than one.
Capacity, contact time, and maintenance matter as much as device choice.

title: Run Basic Household Water Checks and Tests
summary: Inspect and test source or treated water at a household level and record the results in a simple log.
plain_language: A small amount of testing catches many obvious failures before they become a household problem.

payload.performance_statement: Perform a basic set of observable and kit-based checks, document what they mean, and decide whether to store, retreat, or reject the water.
steps_summary: Inspect the sample for clarity, odor, container condition, and labeling.
Run the selected household-level test according to its instructions.
Record date, source, test type, result, and interpretation.
Compare against the chosen threshold or action rule.
Decide whether to store, retreat, re-test, or reject.
success_criteria: The log captures the source, date, method, and decision.
The learner can explain the limit of the chosen test.
The final action is consistent with the result and context.
failure_modes: Using expired or misread test materials.
Drawing conclusions beyond what the kit can show.
Failing to document the sample source and date.
payload.safety_boundary: Household tests are screening tools; if the consequences of error are high, use certified testing and local public-health guidance.

title: Build a 72-Hour and 2-Week Household Water Plan
summary: Produce a practical household water plan covering quantity, storage, treatment, testing, and contingency options.
plain_language: A water plan turns scattered gear and half-remembered ideas into a usable operating system.

payload.goal: Build a household-ready plan that covers how much water is needed, where it comes from, how it is treated, how it is tested, and what to do when one path fails.
acceptance_criteria: The plan covers drinking, hygiene, food prep, pets, and essential cleaning where relevant.
At least one backup source or fallback path is named.
The plan can be followed by another household member without additional explanation.

title: Design a Functional Household Pantry
summary: Design a pantry around calories, diet, shelf life, cooking needs, budget, and storage conditions.
plain_language: A pantry works when it matches what people actually eat, how they cook, and how long they may need it.

payload.goal: Create a pantry layout and stocking plan that matches the household's real diet, budget, storage space, and disruption window.
acceptance_criteria: The design covers staple foods, proteins, fats, seasonings, and special dietary needs where applicable.
Storage conditions and package sizes fit the dwelling and cooking setup.
The output distinguishes minimum stock from aspirational stock.

title: Rotate and Replenish Pantry Stock
summary: Rotate and replenish stored food systematically so stock remains familiar, usable, and current.
plain_language: Stored food is only useful if it stays edible, visible, and part of normal household practice.

payload.performance_statement: Use labels, placement rules, and a simple review schedule to keep pantry stock fresh and aligned to actual use.
steps_summary: Group items by use, shelf life, and replacement frequency.
Place older stock in front and newer stock in back.
Record quantities and trigger points for replenishment.
Review the pantry on a repeating schedule.
Retire, donate, or repurpose items before they become waste.
success_criteria: The household can find and use stock without guesswork.
Review dates and reorder triggers are visible.
Expired or stale items are not accumulating unnoticed.
failure_modes: Buying without a rotation habit.
Storing items that no one regularly cooks or eats.
Failing to set reorder triggers.
payload.safety_boundary: Follow local food-safety guidance and product labeling; when in doubt about safety, discard rather than gamble.

title: Understand Safe Food Preservation Boundaries
summary: Understand the safe boundaries of freezing, drying, fermenting, and canning-related decisions before preserving food.
plain_language: Preservation methods create very different risks; do not treat them as interchangeable.

payload.definition: A conceptual boundary map showing which preservation methods fit which foods, what their limits are, and when guidance must come from authoritative process sources.
key_questions: Which preservation methods are low-risk vs higher-risk?
What environmental factors make preserved food safer or less safe?
When is authoritative process guidance essential?
misconceptions: Any preserved food that looks fine is safe.
A successful batch proves the method was sound.
Drying, fermenting, freezing, and canning have the same risk profile.
formulas_or_rules: Match method to food type and moisture/acid profile.
Use authoritative process guidance for higher-risk methods.
When a method depends on exact procedure, improvisation shrinks safety margin.

title: Start Seeds Reliably
summary: Start plants from seed with correct timing, medium, light, moisture, and labeling.
plain_language: Strong seedlings come from consistent conditions, not from luck.

payload.performance_statement: Start seeds in a controlled way, document the variables, and produce healthy seedlings or a clear diagnosis of what failed.
steps_summary: Choose crops and dates based on local season and transplant window.
Prepare containers, medium, labels, and a light plan.
Sow at the correct depth and moisture level.
Track emergence, watering, and light exposure in a log.
Harden off or re-seed based on results.
success_criteria: Containers are labeled and dated.
The log shows sowing date, crop, and emergence observations.
The learner can explain at least one likely cause if germination or growth is weak.
failure_modes: Starting too early or too late for local conditions.
Under-lighting seedlings and producing weak growth.
Overwatering and damping off.
payload.safety_boundary: Follow seed packet and local climate guidance; avoid introducing contaminated soil or plants into indoor living spaces.

title: Plan a Beginner Garden System
summary: Plan a small bed or container garden for reliable output, maintenance, and learning rather than maximal complexity.
plain_language: A small garden that fits your time and space teaches more than a sprawling one you cannot maintain.

payload.goal: Design a manageable growing system that matches the site, season, water access, labor, and learning goals.
acceptance_criteria: The plan fits the actual sunlight, water access, and available time.
The crop mix includes at least one reliable and one learning-oriented crop.
The layout and calendar are usable without further interpretation.

title: Understand the Building Envelope
summary: Understand how heat, air, moisture, insulation, and leakage pathways affect comfort, cost, and damage.
plain_language: The envelope is the boundary between indoors and outdoors; when it leaks air or traps moisture badly, the whole house pays for it.

payload.definition: A simple model of how heat, air, and moisture move through walls, roofs, floors, windows, and gaps.
key_questions: Where does heat escape or enter most easily?
How do air leaks differ from insulation gaps?
When does moisture turn from nuisance into building damage?
misconceptions: Insulation alone solves all comfort problems.
More sealing is always better even if ventilation is ignored.
formulas_or_rules: Air sealing and insulation solve different but related problems.
Warm air carries moisture; moisture management is part of durability.
Control layers work best when they are continuous.

title: Run a Basic Weatherization Audit
summary: Inspect drafts, heat loss, leakage, and weatherization priorities to rank practical improvements.
plain_language: A weatherization audit tells you where comfort and energy losses are actually happening instead of where you guess they are.

payload.goal: Inspect the building or dwelling for obvious air leakage, weak spots, and easy improvements, then rank them by impact and effort.
acceptance_criteria: The audit identifies where drafts, weak insulation, or leakage are likely.
The output ranks fixes by impact, cost, and reversibility.
The list clearly distinguishes owner-level from renter-friendly actions.

title: Control Moisture and Interrupt Mold Conditions
summary: Identify moisture sources and interrupt the conditions that allow mold and building damage to persist.
plain_language: Mold control starts with moisture control; cleaning alone is not a strategy if the source remains.

payload.performance_statement: Identify likely moisture sources, document conditions that keep surfaces damp, and create a response plan that removes the source or shortens exposure.
steps_summary: Inspect areas near plumbing, exterior walls, windows, bathrooms, kitchens, and basements or crawlspaces.
Classify the moisture source: leak, condensation, infiltration, or occupant load.
Record what keeps the area damp: poor airflow, low heat, trapped vapor, or repeated wetting.
Choose corrective actions that target the source first.
Set a follow-up check date.
success_criteria: The response plan targets the source, not only the surface.
Areas needing escalation are clearly identified.
The learner can explain the difference between leak-driven and condensation-driven problems.
failure_modes: Treating visible mold as only a cleaning problem.
Ignoring intermittent leaks or seasonal condensation patterns.
Failing to recheck after the first intervention.
payload.safety_boundary: Escalate persistent, extensive, or health-sensitive cases to qualified local guidance; do not disturb hazardous materials or hidden cavities without appropriate precautions.

title: Use Essential Hand Tools Confidently
summary: Use measuring, tightening, cutting, fastening, and basic repair tools safely and effectively.
plain_language: Tool literacy lowers the barrier to fixing simple problems before they become expensive ones.

payload.performance_statement: Demonstrate correct selection and basic use of essential hand tools for common household tasks.
steps_summary: Match each tool to the task and material.
Demonstrate measuring, fastening, loosening, cutting, and gripping with control.
Identify common fastener types and the right driver or wrench fit.
Store tools and blades safely after use.
Record gaps in the starter tool list.
success_criteria: The learner uses tools without damaging the fastener or material.
The learner can name when not to force a tool fit.
The checklist shows which core tools are present or still needed.
failure_modes: Using the wrong size driver or wrench.
Applying force before understanding the fastener direction or material.
Neglecting blade safety and hand placement.
payload.safety_boundary: Do not improvise on energized, pressurized, or structurally critical systems when the task exceeds basic hand-tool work.

title: Locate Shutoffs, Seal Gaps, and Make Minor Temporary Repairs
summary: Locate shutoffs, stop minor leaks, seal gaps, and make temporary repairs that buy time and reduce damage.
plain_language: Fast, basic intervention often matters more than elegant intervention in the first minutes of a household problem.

payload.performance_statement: Find the key shutoffs, assemble a small response kit, and apply simple reversible fixes for gaps, drips, and minor surface damage.
steps_summary: Locate water and utility shutoffs and label or map them.
Assemble a small kit for leaks, drafts, and minor patching.
Apply simple sealing or temporary patching to a low-risk target.
Document what worked, what did not, and what still needs escalation.
Store the map and kit where others can find them.
success_criteria: Shutoff locations are recorded and usable under stress.
The patch or seal improves the problem without creating a larger one.
The learner can tell temporary repair from durable repair.
failure_modes: Not knowing the right shutoff until an emergency starts.
Using the wrong sealant or patch for the material or moisture condition.
Treating a temporary fix as a permanent solution.
payload.safety_boundary: Temporary repairs buy time; stop and escalate when structural, electrical, gas, or concealed damage is possible.

title: Understand Basic Circuits and Household Loads
summary: Understand volts, amps, watts, watt-hours, breakers, and common household loads so power choices are realistic.
plain_language: Power problems become much easier once you know the difference between how fast energy is used and how much energy you have stored.

payload.definition: A beginner model of household electrical loads, circuit limits, and the relationship between power and stored energy.
key_questions: What is the difference between watts and watt-hours?
Why do some devices have startup surges?
How do breakers and circuit limits constrain what can run together?
misconceptions: High voltage automatically means high energy use.
Battery capacity alone tells you whether a device will run.
Nameplate numbers always equal real-world operating behavior.
formulas_or_rules: Power is rate; energy is quantity over time.
Add running loads before comparing them to available capacity.
Startup surge and inverter losses reduce effective margin.

title: Run a Critical Loads Audit
summary: Inventory critical devices and outage priorities so backup plans are sized for reality rather than guesswork.
plain_language: You do not need to power everything; you need to know what truly matters and what it costs to keep it running.

payload.goal: Create a ranked list of critical loads, their power draw, duty cycle, and outage priority over different durations.
acceptance_criteria: The audit separates essential, important, and optional loads.
Each listed load includes at least a reasonable wattage estimate and use pattern.
The output is specific enough to size backup options.

title: Estimate Runtime from Stored or Generated Power
summary: Calculate runtime from batteries, generators, or power stations using load, efficiency, and duty-cycle assumptions.
plain_language: Runtime is where wishful thinking meets arithmetic.

payload.performance_statement: Estimate how long a power source can support one or more loads and document the assumptions that change the answer most.
steps_summary: Identify usable energy and convert it into a common unit.
Select one or more loads and estimate whether they run continuously or intermittently.
Account for conversion losses or practical margins.
Calculate runtime for essential scenarios.
Record the assumptions that most affect the result.
success_criteria: The result uses consistent units throughout.
The learner can explain how duty cycle and losses change the answer.
The runtime table is readable by another household member.
failure_modes: Mixing watts and watt-hours.
Ignoring surge, duty cycle, or efficiency losses.
Assuming rated capacity is fully usable in all conditions.
payload.safety_boundary: This is a planning calculation, not a substitute for manufacturer limits, electrical code, or safe generator and battery practices.

title: Draft a Tiered Backup Power Plan
summary: Produce low-, mid-, and high-budget backup options tied to critical loads, runtime goals, and local constraints.
plain_language: A good backup plan gives you options instead of one expensive fantasy setup.

payload.goal: Create a practical backup power plan with tiered options, tradeoffs, and clear matching to critical loads and outage duration goals.
acceptance_criteria: Each tier names what it can and cannot support.
The plan documents assumptions, local constraints, and maintenance burdens.
The decision log explains why one tier is preferred for the current situation.

title: Map Local Skills, Tools, Sources, and Shared Assets
summary: Map local tools, skills, food sources, water sources, spaces, and contacts that matter in normal times and disruptions.
plain_language: Community resilience improves when people know what already exists nearby and who can do what.

payload.goal: Create a local map of people, places, and assets that could support mutual aid, repair, food access, water access, and emergency coordination.
acceptance_criteria: The map includes both physical resources and human skills.
The output distinguishes public, private, and permission-based resources.
Contact or access notes are clear enough to be useful but respectful of privacy.

title: Deliver a Teach-Forward Session
summary: Teach one completed capability to others in a 10–20 minute session using a durable artifact and clear language.
plain_language: Knowledge spreads when someone can pass it on simply, not when they merely possess it privately.

payload.goal: Prepare and deliver a short lesson that transfers one usable capability and leaves behind a handout, checklist, or map that others can reuse.
acceptance_criteria: The lesson has a clear promise, steps, and artifact.
A general audience can follow the language without insider jargon.
At least one participant could use the handout without the instructor present.
